tasks.title: Repository State Analysis & Inventory
tasks.description: Create a complete inventory of the repository, capturing file metadata, dependencies, and entry points to establish a baseline before reorganization.
tasks.details:
Implement a comprehensive repository analysis tool that:

1. Traverses the entire repository structure using `os.walk()` or `pathlib`
2. Calculates MD5 hash for each file to identify duplicates and track changes
3. Extracts file metadata (size, last modified, permissions)
4. Builds a dependency graph by parsing Python imports and JavaScript/TypeScript imports
5. Identifies critical entry points (main.py, app.py, etc.)
6. Detects naming inconsistencies (FreeAgentics vs. cogniticnet)
7. Stores the complete inventory in a structured JSON format

Implementation should use AST parsing for Python files to accurately extract imports rather than regex. For JavaScript/TypeScript, use a proper parser like `esprima` or `babel-parser`.

The output should include:
- Complete file list with metadata
- Dependency graph (what imports what)
- Entry points list
- Potential duplicates
- Naming inconsistencies

This inventory will serve as the foundation for safe reorganization.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Verify inventory completeness by comparing file count with `find . -type f | wc -l`
2. Validate MD5 hash calculation by comparing with system `md5sum` tool
3. Test dependency extraction with known import patterns
4. Verify entry point detection with manual review
5. Test on sample repositories with known structure
6. Ensure the tool handles edge cases like circular imports and dynamic imports

subtasks.title: Repository Traversal Implementation
subtasks.description: Develop a robust module to recursively traverse the repository file system, efficiently handling large codebases and various directory structures. Ensure modularity for easy integration and testability.

subtasks.title: MD5 Hash Calculation for Files
subtasks.description: Implement functionality to compute MD5 hashes for all relevant files discovered during traversal, ensuring accuracy and performance.

subtasks.title: Metadata Extraction Module
subtasks.description: Extract and normalize metadata (e.g., file size, timestamps, language, author if available) for each file and directory in the repository.

subtasks.title: Dependency Graph Construction (Python and JS/TS)
subtasks.description: Parse and analyze Python and JavaScript/TypeScript files to construct accurate dependency graphs, capturing both internal and external dependencies.

subtasks.title: Entry Point Detection
subtasks.description: Identify and catalog entry points (e.g., main scripts, CLI commands, web server initializers) in the repository for supported languages.

subtasks.title: Naming Inconsistency Detection
subtasks.description: Analyze file, directory, and symbol names to detect inconsistencies or violations of naming conventions across the repository.

subtasks.title: Structured JSON Output Generation
subtasks.description: Aggregate all extracted data and analysis results into a well-structured, validated JSON output that meets the PRD and expert committee requirements.

tasks.title: Establish Test Baseline & CI Pipeline
tasks.description: Discover all existing tests, establish a baseline of test coverage, and implement a CI pipeline to ensure changes don't break functionality.
tasks.details:
1. Discover all test files in the repository using pattern matching:
   - Python: `test_*.py`, `*_test.py`
   - JavaScript: `*.spec.js`, `*.test.js`
   - End-to-end tests in dedicated directories

2. Categorize tests by type (unit, integration, e2e)

3. Set up test runners for each language/framework:
   - Python: pytest with coverage
   - JavaScript: Jest with coverage

4. Create a baseline report of:
   - Test count by category
   - Pass/fail status
   - Code coverage percentage
   - Test execution time

5. Implement GitHub Actions workflow for CI

6. Add pre-commit hooks to run tests locally before commits

7. Create a test report dashboard for visibility
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Verify all tests are discovered by comparing with manual inventory
2. Ensure baseline metrics are accurately captured
3. Validate CI pipeline by introducing a passing test and a failing test
4. Test pre-commit hooks with various scenarios
5. Verify coverage reports match expected values

subtasks.title: Automated Test Discovery Implementation
subtasks.description: Develop and configure scripts or tooling to automatically discover all relevant test cases across supported languages and frameworks, ensuring comprehensive coverage and alignment with repository structure.

subtasks.title: Test Categorization and Tagging
subtasks.description: Design and implement a robust system for categorizing and tagging discovered tests by type (e.g., smoke, regression, performance), functional area, and priority, following expert committee guidance.

subtasks.title: Test Runner Setup and Configuration
subtasks.description: Select, configure, and integrate appropriate test runners for each language and framework, ensuring compatibility with the discovery and categorization systems.

subtasks.title: Baseline Reporting Framework Establishment
subtasks.description: Develop a baseline reporting system that aggregates and visualizes test results, supporting categorization, historical trends, and actionable insights for stakeholders.

subtasks.title: Continuous Integration (CI) Pipeline Implementation
subtasks.description: Integrate automated test discovery, categorization, runner execution, and reporting into the CI pipeline, ensuring reliable, repeatable, and scalable test automation as part of the development workflow.

subtasks.title: Pre-commit Hooks and Test Dashboard Setup
subtasks.description: Implement pre-commit hooks to enforce test execution and reporting before code merges, and deploy a real-time dashboard for monitoring test health and trends.

tasks.title: Design New Directory Structure
tasks.description: Create a domain-driven directory structure that properly separates concerns and follows clean architecture principles.
tasks.details: Design a new directory structure following domain-driven design and clean architecture principles with proper layer separation for domain-first organization, clear separation of interface, domain, and infrastructure, configuration separate from code, tests mirror source structure, and documentation as first-class citizen. Create an Architecture Decision Record (ADR) documenting rationale for structure, dependency rules, naming conventions, and file organization principles.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Review structure with team members for feedback
2. Validate against domain model
3. Check that structure accommodates all existing functionality
4. Ensure structure follows clean architecture principles
5. Verify naming consistency throughout

subtasks.title: Requirements Gathering
subtasks.description: Collect and synthesize all relevant requirements from stakeholders, domain experts, and the PRD. Ensure alignment with expert committee guidance (Kent Beck, Robert Martin, Martin Fowler, etc.) and document both functional and non-functional needs.

subtasks.title: Draft Structure Proposal
subtasks.description: Develop a high-level structure for the architecture documentation and ADRs, informed by requirements and best practices. Propose sections, templates, and documentation flow.

subtasks.title: ADR Documentation
subtasks.description: Formalize key architectural decisions using the selected ADR template. Document context, options, rationale, and consequences for each major decision.
subtasks.details:
<info added on 2025-06-20T11:24:09.975Z>
ADR documentation phase completed successfully. Comprehensive architectural decision records (ADRs) have been created for all major system components, including API and interface layer architecture, performance and optimization strategy, developer experience and tooling, security and authentication, and database and persistence strategy. Each ADR includes detailed implementation examples, performance targets, testing strategies, risk mitigation, and clear timelines. All ADRs comply with established directory structure, dependency, and naming conventions, and are fully integrated with prior architectural decisions. subtasks.title: Dependency and Naming Rule Definition
subtasks.description: Define and document rules for architectural dependencies and naming conventions to ensure consistency and maintainability across the project.
subtasks.details:
<info added on 2025-06-20T11:32:08.925Z>
Dependency and naming rule definitions are now fully implemented, with comprehensive tooling and documentation available for developers. The dependency validation guide, practical dependency matrix, and automated checker script (with AST parsing) are accessible at `docs/architecture/dependency-validation-guide.md`. Real-world examples, troubleshooting, and dependency injection patterns are included for clarity. The validation tool (`scripts/validate-dependencies.py`) supports JSON and HTML reporting, detailed violation output, configurable severity, and pre-commit integration. A concise developer quick reference is provided at `docs/architecture/developer-quick-reference.md`, featuring common violation patterns, solutions, and setup instructions. IDE and automation support have been enhanced with updated `.pre-commit-config.yaml` and `.vscode/settings.json`, enabling architectural validation, naming convention checks, and code quality/security enforcement. Enforcement is integrated into pre-commit hooks and CI/CD workflows, with multiple report formats and audit tool compatibility. The current toolset has identified 86 violations (26 domain layer, 60 syntax errors), and ongoing enforcement is in place. These measures ensure all future code is automatically validated against ADR-003 and ADR-004, supporting a clean, maintainable architecture.
</info added on 2025-06-20T11:32:08.925Z>

subtasks.title: Propagate Architectural Constraints to All Pending Tasks
subtasks.description: Systematically iterate through all 'pending' tasks in tasks.json. For each task, prepend a critical directive to its 'details' field, mandating that all file creation and code placement must strictly adhere to the project's canonical structure.

tasks.title: Implement File Movement Plan
tasks.description: Develop and execute a robust plan to move files into the new directory structure, preserving git history and ensuring all functionality remains intact while adhering to architectural decisions.
tasks.details:
1. Review the completed repository inventory and the finalized new directory structure to map each file's current location to its new destination.
2. Use 'git mv' for all file movements to preserve commit history and avoid breaking links to previous changes. For bulk moves, consider scripting with shell or Python to automate 'git mv' operations based on the mapping.
3. Update all import paths, references, and configuration files to reflect the new structure. Use static analysis tools (e.g., pylint, eslint, or custom scripts) to identify and refactor outdated paths.
4. Ensure that all architectural decisions, naming conventions, and dependency rules from the ADR are strictly followed during the move.
5. Commit changes incrementally, using feature branches and protected branch workflows to enable code review and CI validation before merging to main. Avoid direct commits to main and use pull requests for all changes.
6. Communicate the migration plan and timeline to the team, providing documentation and support for resolving merge conflicts or local environment issues.
7. After the move, remove obsolete files and directories, ensuring the repository only contains files necessary for building and running the project.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Run the full test suite and CI pipeline after each batch of file moves to verify that all functionality remains intact and no tests are broken.
2. Manually inspect git history for a sample of moved files to confirm that commit history is preserved.
3. Use static analysis and linting tools to detect broken imports or unresolved references.
4. Validate that the new structure matches the ADR and architectural requirements by reviewing the directory tree and file placements.
5. Confirm that all team members can build, test, and run the project from the new structure without issues.
6. Review pull requests for adherence to naming conventions, dependency rules, and architectural guidelines.

subtasks.title: File Mapping Generation
subtasks.description: Create a comprehensive mapping of current file locations to their intended new destinations, ensuring all files to be moved are accounted for and categorized.
subtasks.details:
Inventory all files in the current structure, document their types and dependencies, and define the target directory structure. Produce a mapping document or script that pairs each source file with its new location.
<info added on 2025-06-20T11:38:31.395Z>
File Mapping Generation successfully completed.

Comprehensive analysis of the entire project structure for ADR-002 compliance has been completed, resulting in the creation of an automated mapping script (`scripts/file-mapping-generator.py`) and a detailed movement plan (`file-mapping.json`). The mapping identifies 22 files for movement into the canonical structure: 10 configuration files to the `config/` directory (infrastructure layer), 2 mock files to `tests/__mocks__/` (testing layer), and 10 infrastructure scripts to `infrastructure/scripts/`. All moves are classified as priority 2 (important but not critical), with no circular dependencies detected and clear layer categorization per ADR-002. The mapping provides a solid foundation for safe, incremental file movement while preserving git history and maintaining architectural compliance. Status: Ready for next phase (Dependency-Based Movement Ordering).
</info added on 2025-06-20T11:38:31.395Z>

subtasks.title: Dependency-Based Movement Ordering
subtasks.description: Analyze file dependencies to determine a safe and logical order for moving files, minimizing the risk of broken references during migration.
subtasks.details:
Parse import statements and references to build a dependency graph. Use this graph to sequence file moves so that dependent files are moved after their dependencies, or batch files with minimal cross-dependencies.
<info added on 2025-06-20T11:40:18.895Z>
Dependency-Based Movement Ordering completed successfully.

Comprehensive dependency analysis was performed using an automated script (`scripts/dependency-movement-order.py`), resulting in a detailed four-batch execution plan documented in `movement-order.json`. The analysis included configuration file cross-dependencies and script references to ensure safe sequencing.

The movement plan consists of four optimized batches, each with clear dependency ordering and incremental validation steps. Estimated total duration is 30-50 minutes, with a 95% success probability and low overall risk. Emergency rollback procedures and Git history preservation strategies are documented.

Batch breakdown:
- Batch 1: 8 independent configuration files, low risk, 5-10 minutes.
- Batch 2: 2 isolated test mock files, low risk, 5 minutes.
- Batch 3: 3 configuration files with cross-dependencies, medium risk, 10-15 minutes (depends on Batch 1).
- Batch 4: 9 infrastructure and deployment scripts, medium risk, 15-20 minutes (depends on Batches 1-3).

Risk assessment confirms 10 low-risk and 12 medium-risk files, with no high-risk files. Each batch includes validation steps and rollback support. The execution strategy uses incremental batch movement, validation between batches, `git mv` for history preservation, and tool functionality checks after each configuration batch.

Status: Ready for the next phase (Import Statement Updating). The dependency analysis ensures a systematic, low-risk approach to file movement and system stability throughout migration.
</info added on 2025-06-20T11:40:18.895Z>

subtasks.title: Import Statement Updating
subtasks.description: Automate the updating of import statements and internal references to reflect new file locations after each move.
subtasks.details:

Comprehensive Reference Update System has been created, including the development of `scripts/update-file-references.py` for automated reference updating, comprehensive pattern matching for various file types and reference patterns, and generation of a `reference-updates.json` report with detailed analysis.

Reference Update Analysis:

- 0 references required updates, confirming minimal cross-dependencies
- 0 errors encountered; all files processed successfully
- Dry run validation completed successfully

Reference Update Capabilities:
- Pattern detection for: package.json script references, VS Code settings, GitHub workflows, Docker files, Makefiles, shell scripts, Python files, TypeScript/JavaScript, and README files
- Pattern-based regex matching for various reference types
- Comprehensive path normalization for consistent comparison
- Backup and rollback support for safety
- Detailed reporting with validation recommendations
- Support for both dry-run and actual execution modes

Validation Results:
- Configuration files: No cross-references detected (as expected)
- Mock files: Isolated test files with no external dependencies
- Infrastructure scripts: Self-contained with minimal external references
- No manual reference updates needed for this migration

Status: All reference patterns analyzed and update mechanism prepared. Since the files being moved (configuration files, mock files, and infrastructure scripts) have minimal cross-references, no actual reference updates are required for this migration. The update system is ready for future migrations involving source code files with more complex import relationships.

Ready for next phase (Git History Preservation).
</info added on 2025-06-20T11:42:12.714Z>

tasks.title: Implement Naming Consistency Across Codebase
tasks.description: Establish and enforce consistent naming conventions for files, variables, classes, and functions throughout the codebase, ensuring strict compliance with architectural decision records (ADRs).
tasks.details:
1. Review the architectural decision records (ADRs) and any existing documentation to extract prescribed naming conventions for files, variables, classes, and functions. 
2. Document a comprehensive naming convention guideline, including examples for each entity type (e.g., UpperCamelCase for classes, snake_case for variables and functions, kebab-case or lower_case for files, as appropriate per language and ADRs). 
3. Audit the entire codebase using static analysis tools and custom scripts to identify deviations from the established conventions. 
4. Refactor all non-compliant names, ensuring changes are atomic and tracked in version control. Use automated refactoring tools where possible to minimize manual errors. 
5. Update all references, imports, and documentation to reflect the new names. 
6. Integrate naming convention checks into the CI pipeline using linters or custom scripts to enforce ongoing compliance. 
7. Communicate the finalized naming guidelines to the development team and update onboarding materials. 
8. Ensure that naming conventions support searchability, clarity, and maintainability, as outlined in best practices.[1][2][3][4][5]
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Run static analysis and linting tools configured with the new naming rules to verify there are no violations across the codebase. 
2. Manually review a representative sample of files, variables, classes, and functions to confirm adherence to the documented conventions. 
3. Execute the full test suite and CI pipeline to ensure that refactoring for naming consistency has not introduced regressions or broken references. 
4. Validate that all documentation and onboarding materials reflect the updated naming conventions. 
5. Confirm that naming convention checks are enforced in the CI pipeline by intentionally introducing violations and verifying that builds fail as expected.

subtasks.title: Define and Document Naming Conventions
subtasks.description: Establish clear, language-appropriate naming conventions for variables, functions, classes, files, and directories. Document these conventions with examples and rationale for team alignment.
subtasks.details:
Include camelCase, PascalCase, snake_case, and kebab-case as appropriate for each language and context. Ensure the documentation is accessible and includes compliant/non-compliant code snippets.
<info added on 2025-06-20T13:05:04.330Z>
Comprehensive naming conventions documentation has been created and integrated into the project. This includes a newly authored CONTRIBUTING.md that provides a detailed developer guide with clear examples for file naming (Python: kebab-case; TypeScript: PascalCase for components, camelCase for utilities), code naming (PascalCase for classes, snake_case for functions/variables), database schema (snake_case), API endpoints (kebab-case), and Git practices (feature branches, conventional commits). The documentation also addresses professional terminology, development workflow, quality standards, and testing guidelines. Visual examples illustrate compliant and non-compliant patterns, ensuring alignment with naming-conventions.json and ADR-004. The documentation is accessible, human-readable, and ready for use in automated tooling.
</info added on 2025-06-20T13:05:04.330Z>

Key Performance Improvements:

- Enhanced error handling prevents script crashes

Testing Results:

- Both scripts: Robust error handling and user-friendly output

All scripts now provide better automation, accuracy, and usability for the naming convention maintenance workflow.

tasks.title: Implement Active Inference Core Engine
tasks.description: Develop a robust, modular Active Inference core that serves as the cognitive engine for agents, strictly adhering to the project's canonical architecture and naming conventions as defined in ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004.
tasks.details:
1. Review ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004 to extract all architectural and naming requirements, ensuring strict compliance throughout the implementation.
2. Architect the Active Inference core as a set of well-defined modules: generative model, inference (state estimation), learning (model parameter updates), and decision-making (policy selection), following the canonical separation of concerns.
3. Implement the generative model to encode agent beliefs about the environment, supporting flexible model structures as per project standards.
4. Develop the inference module to perform Bayesian state estimation, minimizing variational free energy given observations.
5. Implement the learning module to update model parameters based on new data, ensuring compatibility with the project's data flow and update protocols.
6. Build the decision-making module to select actions by minimizing expected free energy, supporting policy evaluation and selection as per the framework.
7. Enforce naming conventions for all classes, functions, and files, referencing the guidelines established in ADRs and the output of the naming consistency task.
8. Integrate comprehensive docstrings and type annotations, and ensure all public APIs are documented according to project standards.
9. Collaborate with architecture and naming convention maintainers to validate compliance before merging.
10. Prepare for extensibility, allowing future enhancements (e.g., hierarchical planning, alternative inference algorithms) without breaking the canonical structure.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Write unit tests for each module (generative model, inference, learning, decision-making) to verify correct mathematical operations and adherence to the Free Energy Principle.
2. Develop integration tests simulating agent-environment interaction, confirming that the agent minimizes expected free energy and updates beliefs/actions as expected.
3. Use static analysis and linting tools configured with the project's naming and architectural rules to ensure strict compliance with ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004.
4. Conduct code reviews with architecture and naming convention maintainers to validate adherence to canonical structure and conventions.
5. Run the full test suite and CI pipeline to ensure no regressions or violations are introduced.
6. Document test coverage and provide example agent scripts demonstrating correct use of the Active Inference core.

tasks.title: Develop Flexible Agent System Architecture
tasks.description: Design and implement a modular, extensible agent system architecture supporting multiple agent types, behaviors, and coalition formation, with strict adherence to ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004.
tasks.details: 1. Review ADR-002 (Canonical Directory Structure), ADR-003 (Dependency Rules), and ADR-004 (Naming Conventions) to extract all architectural, dependency, and naming requirements. 2. Architect the agent system using established design patterns for agentic systems, such as single-agent, multi-agent (swarm/manager), and coalition formation, ensuring modularity and extensibility for new agent types and behaviors. 3. Define clear interfaces and abstract base classes for agent types, behaviors, and coalition logic, enabling plug-and-play extensibility. 4. Implement a flexible agent registry or factory to instantiate agents dynamically based on configuration or runtime needs. 5. Integrate support for tool calling and external action orchestration, as recommended in agent system design patterns, to enable agents to interact with external data sources and services. 6. Ensure all modules, files, and classes strictly follow the canonical directory structure, dependency rules, and naming conventions. 7. Document the architecture, including diagrams and rationale for design choices, and provide usage examples for extending agent types and behaviors.
tasks.testStrategy: 1. Write unit tests for agent instantiation, behavior execution, and coalition formation logic, covering both standard and custom agent types. 2. Develop integration tests simulating multi-agent collaboration and coalition scenarios, verifying correct coordination and extensibility. 3. Use static analysis and linting tools to confirm strict adherence to ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004 across all new modules. 4. Manually review architecture documentation and code structure for compliance with architectural decisions. 5. Validate extensibility by implementing and testing at least one new agent type and coalition behavior as a proof of concept.

tasks.title: Implement API and Interface Layer
tasks.description: Develop a clean, well-documented API layer for the agent system, providing REST endpoints, WebSocket support for real-time updates, and optional GraphQL, all strictly adhering to canonical architecture, naming, and dependency rules.
tasks.details:
1. Review the canonical directory structure, naming conventions, and dependency rules as defined in the relevant ADRs to ensure strict compliance throughout the API and interface layer implementation.
2. Design and implement RESTful endpoints for all major agent system operations (e.g., agent creation, state queries, action dispatch), ensuring statelessness and clear resource modeling.
3. Integrate WebSocket support to enable real-time updates and event-driven communication between clients and the agent system, handling connection lifecycle, authentication, and message routing according to best practices.
4. Optionally, implement a GraphQL endpoint to provide flexible, efficient data querying for clients with complex or dynamic requirements, following GraphQL best practices for schema design, authorization, and error handling.
5. Ensure all API routes, handlers, and schemas are placed in the correct directories and named according to project conventions.
6. Provide comprehensive inline documentation and generate external API documentation (e.g., OpenAPI/Swagger for REST, GraphQL schema docs) to facilitate integration and maintenance.
7. Coordinate with the agent system architecture to ensure seamless integration and clear separation of concerns between interface and core logic.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Write unit and integration tests for all REST endpoints, WebSocket handlers, and GraphQL resolvers, verifying correct request/response formats, error handling, and adherence to statelessness (for REST).
2. Simulate real-time client interactions over WebSockets, testing connection management, authentication, and event delivery.
3. Validate GraphQL queries and mutations against the schema, ensuring correct data retrieval, mutation, and authorization.
4. Use static analysis and linting tools to confirm compliance with directory structure, naming conventions, and dependency rules.
5. Review generated API documentation for completeness and accuracy.
6. Perform end-to-end tests by integrating the API layer with the agent system and verifying all major workflows from the client perspective.

tasks.title: Create Comprehensive Project Documentation
tasks.description: Develop complete, high-quality documentation for the project, including README, API docs, architecture diagrams, tutorials, and examples, ensuring strict compliance with ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004. All documentation must reside exclusively in the docs/ directory.
tasks.details:
1. Review ADR-002 (canonical project structure), ADR-003 (dependency rules), and ADR-004 (naming conventions) to extract all documentation requirements and compliance criteria.
2. Structure the docs/ directory to include: a detailed README, API documentation (auto-generated where possible), architecture diagrams (using tools like PlantUML or Mermaid), step-by-step tutorials, and real-world usage examples.
3. Use clear headings, subheadings, and a logical navigation structure for all documents[3][5].
4. Visualize complex concepts with diagrams and flowcharts, embedding them directly in the documentation[3].
5. Include troubleshooting guides, FAQs, and a glossary of technical terms for accessibility[3][5].
6. Ensure all documentation uses inclusive, plain language and is accessible to both technical and non-technical audiences[5].
7. Leverage modern documentation tools (e.g., Markdown, Sphinx, MkDocs, or Docusaurus) for maintainability and version control[2][4].
8. Collaborate with subject-matter experts and developers to ensure technical accuracy and completeness[5].
9. Validate that all documentation files are placed exclusively in the docs/ directory, following naming conventions and directory structure as mandated by the ADRs.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Review the docs/ directory to confirm all required documentation types (README, API docs, diagrams, tutorials, examples) are present and complete.
2. Validate that all documentation strictly adheres to ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004 by cross-referencing content and structure.
3. Use automated tools to check for broken links, missing sections, and formatting issues.
4. Solicit feedback from developers and end users to ensure clarity, accuracy, and usefulness of the documentation[5].
5. Verify that no documentation exists outside the docs/ directory and that all files follow the prescribed naming conventions and structure.

tasks.title: Implement Developer Experience Improvements
tasks.description: Enhance the developer experience by providing intuitive APIs, robust development tools, advanced debugging capabilities, and comprehensive usage examples, ensuring strict compliance with ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004.
tasks.details:
1. Review ADR-002 (Canonical Directory Structure), ADR-003 (Dependency Rules), and ADR-004 (Naming Conventions) to extract all developer-facing requirements and compliance criteria.
2. Refactor and document all public APIs for clarity, consistency, and ease of use, ensuring method signatures, parameter names, and return types are intuitive and follow established naming conventions.
3. Develop or integrate developer tools such as CLI utilities, code generators, and configuration wizards to streamline common development workflows (e.g., project scaffolding, environment setup, code linting, and formatting).
4. Implement advanced debugging capabilities, including detailed error messages, stack traces, and optional verbose logging, ensuring all debug output adheres to naming and directory structure conventions.
5. Create a suite of comprehensive, real-world code examples and usage patterns, placing them in the appropriate canonical locations as defined by ADR-002, and cross-linking them in the documentation.
6. Ensure all improvements are modular, maintainable, and do not violate dependency rules (ADR-003), and that all new files, classes, and functions strictly follow naming conventions (ADR-004).
7. Collaborate with the documentation team to ensure all new tools, APIs, and debugging features are thoroughly documented and discoverable.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Conduct developer usability testing sessions to gather feedback on API intuitiveness, tool effectiveness, and debugging clarity.
2. Write and execute automated tests for all new CLI tools, code generators, and debugging utilities, verifying correct operation and error handling.
3. Review all new and refactored code for strict adherence to ADR-002, ADR-003, and ADR-004 using static analysis, linting, and manual inspection.
4. Validate that all code examples are functional, up-to-date, and correctly placed within the canonical directory structure.
5. Solicit feedback from at least two developers new to the project, ensuring they can onboard and use the APIs and tools with minimal friction.

tasks.title: Integrate Legacy src/ Directory into Canonical Structure
tasks.description: Analyze and migrate all code from the legacy src/ directory into the canonical directory structure defined in ADR-002, ensuring domain separation, preservation of git history, and updating all codebase references.
tasks.details:
1. Conduct a comprehensive analysis of the legacy src/ directory, mapping all files, modules, and dependencies to their appropriate locations in the new canonical structure as defined in ADR-002 and the output of Task 3. 
2. For each code unit, determine its domain and layer (domain, interface, infrastructure, etc.) to ensure proper separation and alignment with clean architecture principles.
3. Use git's 'filter-repo' or 'subtree' tools to migrate files while preserving commit history, documenting the process for traceability.
4. Refactor code as needed to comply with new naming conventions (per Task 5) and resolve any architectural or dependency issues that arise from the move.
5. Update all import paths, configuration files, build scripts, and documentation to reference the new locations.
6. Coordinate with stakeholders to identify and address any business-critical logic or legacy behaviors that must be preserved, as recommended by migration best practices[3][5].
7. Perform incremental migrations, starting with low-risk modules, and validate each step before proceeding to minimize disruption and risk[5].
8. Maintain a detailed migration log and rollback plan to ensure recoverability in case of issues.
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Verify that all legacy src/ code is present in the new canonical structure, with commit history intact (using git log and file comparison tools).
2. Run the full test suite to ensure no regressions or broken dependencies after migration.
3. Manually review a sample of migrated modules to confirm correct domain placement and adherence to architectural and naming conventions.
4. Validate that all references, imports, and build scripts correctly point to the new locations.
5. Confirm with stakeholders that all business-critical functionality is preserved and operational post-migration.
6. Review the migration log for completeness and accuracy, and test rollback procedures on a sample module.
